description: This figure presents 8 optical micrographs of as-cast Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys with Mn content of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 mass% respectively, each with a 100 μm scale bar. The microstructures exhibit typical dendritic solidification features, where the bright phase corresponds to ferrite (α) and the dark region corresponds to austenite (γ). A clear trend is observed: the volume fraction of α phase decreases continuously as Mn content increases, and the alloy with 40 mass% Mn shows nearly single γ phase microstructure. These observations confirm Mn acts as an austenite stabilizer in this alloy system, and provide direct microstructure evidence for the correlation between Mn content, phase composition and mechanical properties of the Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy series.

This is a bright-field transmission electron micrograph of Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy, with a scale bar of 4.0 μm. Region labeled A corresponds to D0₃ ordered α-ferrite phase, region labeled B corresponds to γ-austenite phase, and the dark precipitates marked as κ'' are coarse (Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ carbides with L1₂ ordered structure. The κ'' precipitates are mainly distributed at the α/γ phase boundaries and inside the D0₃ matrix, and the content of κ'' precipitates at phase boundaries is higher than that in the D0₃ matrix interior. This figure demonstrates the duplex phase microstructure feature of the studied alloy, provides clear region references for subsequent phase structure and orientation relationship characterization, and supports the conclusion that the Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy with 15 mass% Mn consists of (D0₃ + κ'') and (γ + κ + κ') mixed phase regions.

This is a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern collected from the coarse κ'' precipitate in Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys, with diffraction spots indexed as 000, 010, 020, 100, 110, 120, 200, 210, 220, where the 020, 220 and 200 spots are underlined. The pattern confirms that the κ'' phase is (Fe, Mn)₃AlCₓ carbide with an ordered L1₂-type structure, having a lattice parameter of a = 0.387–0.389 nm. The similar intensities of superlattice spots (100, 110, 120) indicate that this carbide forms at high temperature with more random, disordered carbon distribution. This figure supports the crystal structure identification of coarse κ'' precipitates, providing direct experimental evidence for the precipitate evolution rule in Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy systems.

This set of figures presents TEM characterization results of the austenite (γ) phase region in Fe-10Al-15Mn-1.0C (Alloy C) sheet casting. The first sub-figure is a bright-field TEM micrograph with a 0.5 μm scale bar, showing two types of (Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ (κ) carbide precipitates in the γ matrix: relatively coarse precipitates in region C and ultra-fine precipitates in region D. The second and third sub-figures are [001] zone-axis selected area electron diffraction patterns collected from region C and D respectively, where superlattice spots besides γ matrix spots are observed, with much stronger 100 superlattice spot intensity than 110, confirming the L'1₂ ordered structure of κ carbides; satellites around 200 and 220 spots indicate the precipitates form via spinodal decomposition. The fourth sub-figure is a dark-field TEM micrograph (0.5 μm scale bar) taken with 100 κ phase reflection, visualizing the distribution of nano-sized κ precipitates and confirming identical orientation of κ and ultra-fine κ' precipitates. These results support that the γ phase in the studied Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy contains L'1₂ structured κ carbides formed by spinodal decomposition during cooling.

This set of figures contains two selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns and two TEM dark-field micrographs targeting the D03 ordered phase in Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys. The SAED patterns are indexed with typical diffraction spots including 000, 200, 220, 111 series spots to confirm the crystal structure of the D03 phase. The g=111 and g=200 dark-field micrographs, both equipped with a 0.5 μm scale bar, reveal the morphology of D03 domains and anti-phase boundary microstructures in the ferrite phase region. These characterization results verify that the α ferrite phase in the studied alloy system is the D03 ordered phase mixed with coarse (Fe,Mn)3AlCx κ carbides, supporting the reported phase composition and evolution rule that α phase fraction decreases with rising Mn content in this Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy system.

This is a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern acquired from the D03 ordered ferrite matrix and κ'' (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbide phase in Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C casting alloys studied in the work. Diffraction spots belonging to the D03 matrix are marked with dashed lines, with indexed crystallographic planes including 000, 220, 400, and 200; diffraction spots belonging to the κ phase are marked with solid lines, with indexed planes including 111, 11-1, and 200. This pattern verifies that the orientation relationship between the D03 matrix and κ'' carbide is [1-11]_κ parallel to [220]_D03 and (011)_κ parallel to (001)_D03, which supports the conclusion that the κ'' precipitates exhibit specific orientation matching with the D03 matrix in the investigated Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy system.

This set of plots presents the mechanical properties of Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy sheet castings, where x ranges from 5 to 40 mass%, corresponding to Alloy A to Alloy H respectively. The first subplot shows the variation of ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa) and yield strength (YS, MPa) with Mn content: both UTS and YS first increase, reaching maximum values of ~887 MPa and ~824 MPa at Alloy C (15 mass% Mn), then decrease gradually as Mn content further increases. The second subplot displays changes in elongation (EL, %) and Rockwell hardness (HRC): hardness follows the same trend as strength, peaking at ~43.3 HRC at Alloy C, while elongation continuously rises with increasing Mn content, from ~16.3% for Alloy A to ~29.2% for Alloy H. These results demonstrate that Mn content effectively regulates the mechanical performance of this alloy system, enabling tunable strength-ductility matching via Mn content adjustment.

This set of three scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images displays the fracture surface morphologies of Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys with varied Mn contents. The first image with a 60 μm scale bar corresponds to the Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy, the second with a 4.0 μm scale bar corresponds to the Fe–10Al–25Mn–1.0C alloy, and the third with a 1 μm scale bar corresponds to the Fe–10Al–35Mn–1.0C alloy. As Mn content increases from 15 mass% to 35 mass%, the fracture features evolve alongside the reported reduction in ferrite (α) phase fraction and improvement of alloy ductility: the 15Mn alloy presents more brittle fracture features matching its higher strength and lower elongation, while the 35Mn alloy shows finer ductile fracture characteristics consistent with its 27.3% elongation and lower strength. These observations verify the relationship between Mn-modulated phase composition and mechanical properties of the Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy system.

This set of 8 plots displays the frequency response function (FRF) magnitude curves of Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys with Mn contents of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, and 40 mass% respectively, obtained via experimental modal analysis. The x-axis is linear frequency ranging from 0 to 1.6 kHz, and the y-axis is FRF magnitude (in dB, unit mass·s⁻²N⁻¹). Each curve shows two characteristic peaks: a low-frequency peak at 226–242 Hz corresponding to the sample natural frequency, and a high-frequency peak around 1.4 kHz. With Mn content increasing from 5 to 40 mass%, the natural frequency shows a slight upward trend, while the damping ratio reflected by the low-frequency peak width decreases gradually, consistent with the trend that damping reduces as Mn content rises and α phase fraction drops. These curves are used to calculate Young's modulus and damping ratio, supporting that Mn regulates dynamic mechanical properties of the alloys by modifying phase composition.

This chart presents the variation of damping ratio as a function of manganese content for Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C sheet cast alloys, with Mn content ranging from 5 mass% to 40 mass%. The x-axis is Manganese Content (mass%) ranging 5 to 40, and the y-axis is dimensionless Damping Ratio ranging 0 to 0.07. The plotted data points demonstrate a continuous monotonic decreasing trend: damping ratio decreases from approximately 0.0603 at 5 mass% Mn to approximately 0.0417 at 40 mass% Mn, which are extracted from frequency response functions via the half-power point method. The decreasing trend correlates with the reduced fraction of ferrite (α) phase (containing D03 ordered structure and coarse κ carbides) as Mn content increases, since the α phase contributes to higher damping capacity. This figure validates that Mn content is an effective parameter to tune the damping performance of the Fe-Al-Mn-C alloy system.
